statement: The radical of an ideal is the intersection of all prime ideals containing it.
proof:
Proof: let `P` be the set of prime ideals `J` with `I ≤ J`. First, if `x ∈ radical I`, then
`x^n ∈ I` for some `n`. For any such prime `J`, we have `x^n ∈ J`, and prime ideals are radical, so
`x ∈ J`. Hence `x` lies in `sInf P`, i.e. in the intersection of all primes containing `I`.

Conversely, if `x ∉ radical I`, then no power of `x` lies in `I`. Consider the multiplicative set
`S = {1, x, x^2, ...}`. It is disjoint from `I`. A standard maximality argument shows there exists
an ideal `J` containing `I` that is maximal among ideals disjoint from `S`; such a `J` is prime and
still disjoint from `S`, so in particular `x ∉ J`. Thus `x` is not in the intersection of all prime
ideals containing `I`. This proves equality.
